bibliographic_data.title: Understanding Server-Assisted Federated Learning in the Presence of Incomplete Client Participation
bibliographic_data.abstract: Existing works in federated learning (FL) often assume an ideal system with either full client or uniformly distributed client participation. However, it has been observed that some clients may never participate in FL training due to a myriad of system heterogeneity factors. A popular approach to mitigate the impacts of incomplete client participation is the server-assisted federated learning (SA-FL) framework, where the server is equipped with an auxiliary dataset. Despite SA-FL being empirically shown to be effective, there remains a lack of theoretical understanding for SA-FL. This paper rigorously investigates SA-FL by first showing that conventional FL is not PAC-learnable under incomplete client participation in the worst case and then demonstrating that the PAC-learnability of FL with incomplete client participation can indeed be revived by SA-FL. Lastly, the SAFARI (server-assisted federated averaging) algorithm is proposed to provide practical guidance for SA-FL training under incomplete client participation, offering the first SA-FL algorithm with convergence guarantee.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper rigorously investigates server-assisted federated learning (SA-FL) by showing that conventional FL is not PAC-learnable under incomplete client participation and proposing the SAFARI algorithm with convergence guarantees.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use PAC learning to establish a worst-case generalization error lower bound for classic FL, proving a new generalization error bound for SA-FL, and propose the SAFARI (server-assisted federated averaging) algorithm.
outcomes: Rigorous theoretical justification of the use of SA-FL by showing that it can revive the PAC learnability of FL with incomplete client participation.
Proposal of the SAFARI algorithm with provable convergence rates matching state-of-the-art classic FL algorithms.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Federated learning (FL) has emerged as a powerful distributed learning paradigm, but faces challenges such as system heterogeneity and incomplete client participation. This paper investigates server-assisted federated learning (SA-FL), where the server is equipped with an auxiliary dataset to mitigate these issues. The authors first show that conventional FL cannot achieve PAC learnability under worst-case scenarios of incomplete client participation. They then demonstrate that SA-FL can revive this learnability, and propose the SAFARI algorithm for practical training guidance. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of SAFARI.
